primary_diagnosis.name: Acute non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (early acute respiratory distress syndrome) secondary to aspiration

pathophysiology_summary: A witnessed aspiration event has driven an acute inflammatory lung injury: chemical/aspiration pneumonitis progressing to diffuse alveolar damage and increased alveolar-capillary permeability. Protein-rich fluid floods the alveoli despite NORMAL left-heart filling pressures, so this is NON-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema — the picture of early ARDS (Berlin definition: acute onset within a week of a known insult, bilateral infiltrates, respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload). The result is refractory hypoxaemia (shunt physiology, hence SpO2 84% that does not correct with rest/position), high work of breathing and impending respiratory-muscle fatigue. The absence of raised JVP, S3 gallop, peripheral oedema and any cardiac history — plus expected normal BNP and a normal heart size on chest X-ray with bilateral patchy alveolar infiltrates — is what separates this from cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, and is why diuretics/GTN (the reflex heart-failure treatment) do not work here. Definitive care is hospital respiratory support (high-flow oxygen, likely intubation and mechanical ventilation in ICU) while the lung injury resolves; the pre-hospital priority is oxygen and airway support via emergency ambulance.

differential.evidence_basis: Refractory severe hypoxaemia and air-hunger developing over 12–36 h directly after a witnessed aspiration event, with no cardiac history.

differential.diagnosis: Cardiogenic (acute) pulmonary oedema / acute heart failure
differential.evidence_basis: Diffuse crackles and severe breathlessness could reflex-anchor to heart failure — but no JVP elevation, no S3, no peripheral oedema, no orthopnoea history, no relief on sitting up, and no cardiac history argue against it. This is the key discrimination and the anchoring trap.
Fever 38.1, recent aspiration and antibiotics; overlaps with and can drive the same lung injury — the two are a continuum here rather than mutually exclusive.
Acute hypoxaemic dyspnoea in a bedbound patient — but diffuse bilateral crackles and the clear temporal link to aspiration make a large PE less likely; not excludable without imaging.
Diffuse bilateral crackles rather than stony dullness and diminished basal breath sounds argue against a large effusion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the safety-critical move is recognising life-threatening refractory hypoxia and escalating; distinguishing non-cardiogenic from cardiogenic aetiology is the diagnostic-reasoning test but must NOT delay 000.

differential.evidence_basis: Refractory hypoxia + dusky lips + drowsiness (hypoxaemic encephalopathy) after aspiration, with a clean cardiac history, fits diffuse alveolar injury, not fluid overload.

differential.diagnosis: Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema / acute heart failure
differential.evidence_basis: Kept on the list because it cannot be excluded remotely, but the absent JVP/S3/oedema and lack of cardiac history make it unlikely — critically, treating it as heart failure with a diuretic is harmful here.
Fever and recent aspiration keep infective lung injury firmly in play as the driver of the ARDS picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should NOT commit to a single aetiology remotely; it should recognise refractory hypoxaemic respiratory failure and escalate, explicitly avoiding the heart-failure/diuretic anchor.

differential.diagnosis: Non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema / early ARDS secondary to aspiration (hospital-confirmed picture: normal BNP, normal heart size, bilateral patchy alveolar infiltrates)
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed only with investigations unavailable in telehealth (BNP, chest imaging, ABG). Remotely the correct end-state is 'life-threatening refractory hypoxaemic respiratory failure — emergency transfer', not a locked aetiology.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth ceiling: the aetiological confirmation is a hospital determination; the AI Doctor is judged on escalation, not on naming ARDS.

red_flags.description: Refractory hypoxaemia — home oximeter SpO2 84% not improving with rest in a well-ventilated room
red_flags.clinical_significance: Oxygen saturation in the low 80s reflects critical hypoxaemia and shunt physiology; it will not correct without high-concentration oxygen and ventilatory support and predicts imminent decompensation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal hypoxaemic respiratory failure — delaying transport risks respiratory arrest.

red_flags.description: Dusky/cyanotic lips with new drowsiness/confusion — end-organ (cerebral) effect of severe hypoxia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Central cyanosis plus altered mental state signals oxygen delivery failing to meet demand and impending collapse.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to loss of consciousness and respiratory/cardiac arrest.

red_flags.description: Severe respiratory distress with exhaustion / respiratory-muscle fatigue — barely able to speak
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fatigue in the setting of high work of breathing precedes hypoventilation and arrest; a 'quietening' patient is a peri-arrest patient.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognised fatigue leads to sudden respiratory arrest.

red_flags.description: Rapid escalation over 12 hours with no response to positioning — trajectory red flag
red_flags.clinical_significance: A steep worsening curve unresponsive to simple measures marks a self-progressing critical illness needing emergency intervention now.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: False reassurance and delay while the patient deteriorates.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Seeing 'severe breathlessness + diffuse crackles' and locking onto acute heart failure, then recommending a diuretic (frusemide) or GTN. In non-cardiogenic oedema the left heart is fine — diuresis does not treat the leaky alveolar membrane and can harm by dropping preload in a warm-shock/inflammatory state.

Settling on 'pneumonia, keep taking antibiotics at home' and missing that the patient is in critical refractory hypoxaemic failure needing emergency oxygen and ventilatory support regardless of the infective label.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a home oximeter
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Discounting an 84% reading as 'probably a bad probe/cold finger'. With visible distress, dusky lips and drowsiness the reading is corroborated and must be treated as real until proven otherwise.

Attempting to titrate treatment or arrange next-day review instead of escalating. The needed interventions (high-flow oxygen, likely intubation and ICU ventilation) cannot be delivered remotely — the only correct remote act is immediate 000.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Jugular venous pressure (flat JVP) — requires bedside inspection
Cardiac auscultation for an S3 gallop or new murmur
Chest auscultation for the distribution of crackles
Presence/absence of peripheral pitting oedema and peripheral perfusion (warm bounding pulses)
Arterial blood gas / true PaO2 and CO2
Chest radiograph (heart size, bilateral alveolar infiltrates)
Measured blood pressure and precise respiratory rate
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 for an emergency ambulance (Intensive Care Paramedic capability if available) stating a critically hypoxic patient in severe respiratory distress; keep him sitting upright and calm, minimise exertion/talking, stay with him, unlock the door, and be ready to start CPR if he stops breathing or becomes unresponsive. Paramedics provide high-flow oxygen and advanced airway/ventilatory support en route to a tertiary ICU.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High for the AETIOLOGY (cardiogenic vs non-cardiogenic vs infective cannot be resolved remotely) — but the ESCALATION decision is unambiguous. The danger is anchoring on heart failure and advising a diuretic, or on 'chest infection' and advising home antibiotics, either of which delays emergency transfer.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute (≤1 week) hypoxaemic respiratory failure with bilateral opacities not fully explained by cardiac failure/fluid overload defines ARDS; an objective assessment (e.g. echocardiography/BNP) is used to exclude a cardiogenic cause.
If the patient becomes unresponsive and not breathing normally, bystanders should call 000 and commence chest compressions immediately with dispatcher assistance.
